title: How pclmbayes compares with the ungroup package
text: pclmbayes popular ungroup package implement penalised composite link models (PCLM) redistributing wide-bin counts onto fine grid. share high-level idea — B-splines plus difference penalty plus composite-link C matrix — implement different statistical models different likelihoods, links, identifiability conventions, supported bin layouts. vignette spells differences , compares three algorithms shipped pclmbayes (pclm(), bpclm(), pclm_exact()) ungroup::pclm() synthetic data known truth two real datasets distributed package.
code: library(pclmbayes)

title: How pclmbayes compares with the ungroup package
text: short: ungroup Poisson smoother grouped counts, pclmbayes multinomial density estimator Bayesian variant exact-ungrouping option. two solve overlapping distinct problems; numerical results agree closely shape fitted distribution, disagree O(shrinkage) bin totals.

title: How pclmbayes compares with the ungroup package
text: contrasting ungroup, recap pclmbayes entry point (use multinomial / softmax model): pclm() — frequentist penalised scoring (Newton/IRLS) maximises penalised multinomial log-likelihood. smoothing parameter τ\tau selected BIC default grid. Returns point estimate ϕ̂\hat\phi asymptotic variance-covariance matrix inverse penalised Fisher information. bpclm() — fully Bayesian: MALA proposal ϕ\phi adaptive step size targeting 0.57 acceptance, Gibbs draw τ\tau Gamma full conditional. Returns chain ϕ\phi, π\pi τ\tau. Optionally enforces unimodality, log-concavity, monotonicity rejecting proposals violate (Eq. 7 Lambert & Eilers). pclm_exact() — constrained MAP. Solves min⁡ϕ12ϕ′Pϕ\min_\phi \tfrac12\,\phi'P\phi subject Cπ(ϕ)=m/m+C\pi(\phi) = m / m_+ exactly, sequential quadratic programming Lagrangian. smoothness penalty objective; bin counts reproduced machine precision. canonical “ungrouping” point estimate. three differ trade . pclm() bpclm() trade fit--bins smoothness τ\tau; pclm_exact() hits bins exactly minimises residual roughness. bpclm() adds posterior uncertainty.

what: 3. Synthetic comparison: 5-year mortality bands → single years
title: How pclmbayes compares with the ungroup package
text: canonical demographic ungrouping use case: know truth (single-year deaths), aggregate 5-year bands, recover single-year breakdown. measure RMSE per single-year cell truth, max absolute residual wide-bin totals, runtime.
code: ages   <- 0:99 true_p <- 0.10 * dnorm(ages, mean =  2, sd =  3) +           0.06 * dnorm(ages, mean = 25, sd = 12) +           0.84 * dnorm(ages, mean = 75, sd = 12) true_p <- true_p / sum(true_p) N      <- 50000L true_yearly <- as.numeric(rmultinom(1L, size = N, prob = true_p)) year_to_band <- (ages %/% 5L) + 1L m_band <- as.numeric(tapply(true_yearly, year_to_band, sum)) wb     <- cbind(seq(0, 95, by = 5L), seq(5, 100, by = 5L))

previous_headings: 3. Synthetic comparison: 5-year mortality bands → single years
title: How pclmbayes compares with the ungroup package
code: t_pclm <- system.time(   fit_pclm <- pclm(m = m_band, wide_breaks = wb,                    a = 0, b = 100, ngrid = 100L,                    ndx = 22L, degree = 3L, penalty_order = 3L) )[["elapsed"]]  t_pclm_exact <- system.time(   fit_exact <- pclm_exact(m = m_band, wide_breaks = wb,                           a = 0, b = 100, ngrid = 100L,                           ndx = 22L, degree = 3L, penalty_order = 3L) )[["elapsed"]]  t_bpclm <- system.time(   fit_b <- bpclm(m = m_band, wide_breaks = wb,                  a = 0, b = 100, ngrid = 100L,                  ndx = 22L, degree = 3L, penalty_order = 3L,                  niter = 3000L, burnin = 1000L, adapt = 500L,                  seed = 7L) )[["elapsed"]] fit_b_cal <- calibrate(fit_b)             # exact band totals t_ungroup <- system.time(   fit_ung <- ungroup::pclm(x = wb[, 1L], y = m_band,                            nlast = 5, out.step = 1) )[["elapsed"]] ung_yearly <- as.numeric(fit_ung$fitted)  # length 100, single-year estimates

previous_headings: 3. Synthetic comparison: 5-year mortality bands → single years
title: How pclmbayes compares with the ungroup package
text: expected pattern: pclm() — multinomial smoother — non-zero max bin residual (smoothness penalty pulls bin probabilities away empirical proportions). per-cell RMSE competitive wrong tool want exact totals. pclm_exact() bpclm() + calibrate() reproduce bin totals machine precision. right tool ungrouping. ungroup::pclm() — Poisson smoother — also tends small non-zero bin residual (Poisson PCLM smoothed fit, constrained one). RMSE values across four typically within 5–10% size data; per-cell shape dominated irreducible multinomial-within-band sampling noise order nj/6\sqrt{n_j / 6} 5-year band.
code: yhat_pclm  <- fit_pclm$pi  * sum(m_band) yhat_exact <- fit_exact$pi * sum(m_band) yhat_bpclm <- fit_b_cal$pi * sum(m_band)  rmse <- function(a, b) sqrt(mean((a - b) ^ 2))  # Wide-bin residual after re-aggregating the single-year fit band_resid <- function(yhat) {   band <- as.numeric(tapply(yhat, year_to_band, sum))   max(abs(band - m_band)) }  results <- data.frame(   algorithm   = c("pclm",          "bpclm + calibrate", "pclm_exact",                   "ungroup::pclm"),   RMSE_yearly = c(rmse(yhat_pclm,  true_yearly),                   rmse(yhat_bpclm, true_yearly),                   rmse(yhat_exact, true_yearly),                   rmse(ung_yearly, true_yearly)),   max_bin_residual = c(band_resid(yhat_pclm),                        band_resid(yhat_bpclm),                        band_resid(yhat_exact),                        band_resid(ung_yearly)),   elapsed_sec = c(t_pclm, t_bpclm, t_pclm_exact, t_ungroup) ) results #>           algorithm RMSE_yearly max_bin_residual elapsed_sec #> 1              pclm    44.79191     7.537445e+01       0.021 #> 2 bpclm + calibrate    34.84219     4.547474e-13       0.306 #> 3        pclm_exact    27.36794     1.818989e-12       0.026 #> 4     ungroup::pclm    78.29337     1.392698e+00       0.113

previous_headings: 3. Synthetic comparison: 5-year mortality bands → single years
title: How pclmbayes compares with the ungroup package
text: Single-year fits four algorithms vs. simulated truth. Visually four nearly indistinguishable body distribution; perceptible differences boundaries (Poisson softmax tails fall slightly differently) inside bins pclm_exact() lacks small kinks calibrate() can introduce.
code: plot(ages, true_yearly, type = "h", col = "grey70",      xlab = "Age (years)", ylab = "Deaths",      main = "Recovered single-year counts") lines(ages, yhat_exact, col = "black",     lwd = 2) lines(ages, yhat_pclm,  col = "steelblue", lwd = 2, lty = 2) lines(ages, yhat_bpclm, col = "firebrick", lwd = 2, lty = 3) if (have_ungroup) {   lines(ages, ung_yearly, col = "darkgreen", lwd = 2, lty = 4)   legend("topleft", bty = "n",          legend = c("Truth", "pclm_exact", "pclm",                     "bpclm + calibrate", "ungroup::pclm"),          col    = c("grey70", "black", "steelblue",                     "firebrick", "darkgreen"),          lwd    = c(1, 2, 2, 2, 2),          lty    = c(1, 1, 2, 3, 4)) } else {   legend("topleft", bty = "n",          legend = c("Truth", "pclm_exact", "pclm", "bpclm + calibrate"),          col    = c("grey70", "black", "steelblue", "firebrick"),          lwd    = c(1, 2, 2, 2),          lty    = c(1, 1, 2, 3)) }

previous_headings: 3. Synthetic comparison: 5-year mortality bands → single years
title: How pclmbayes compares with the ungroup package
text: pclm(), pclm_exact() ungroup::pclm() point estimates. four, bpclm() produces posterior chain densities , calibrate(), preserve bin totals exactly — posterior predictive intervals single-year resolution come free. ungroup::pclm() returns delta-method confidence intervals based asymptotic variance Poisson PCLM, constraint resulting interval respect band totals. Posterior predictive 90% PI bpclm + calibrate.
code: pp <- posterior_predict(fit_b_cal, type = "predictive",                         level = 0.9, seed = 7L) plot(pp, show_bins = FALSE,      xlab = "Age (years)", ylab = "Single-year deaths",      main = "bpclm + calibrate: 90% posterior PI") lines(ages, true_yearly, col = "grey50", lwd = 1.4, lty = 2)

title: How pclmbayes compares with the ungroup package
text: Seven irregular wide bins blood-lead concentration, n=139n = 139. ground truth — visualise recovered density. Recovered density bloodlead data, four algorithms. pclm_exact reproduces seven observed counts machine precision; pclm() bpclm() shrink largest bin ~10% toward smooth fit; ungroup::pclm() also smoothed fit similar (generally smaller, owing Poisson rather multinomial likelihood) shrinkage pattern.
code: data(bloodlead) wb_b <- with(bloodlead, cbind(lower, upper))  fit_b_pclm  <- pclm(      m = bloodlead$count, wide_breaks = wb_b,                           a = 0, b = 80, ngrid = 80L, ndx = 17L,                           degree = 3L, penalty_order = 3L) fit_b_exact <- pclm_exact(m = bloodlead$count, wide_breaks = wb_b,                           a = 0, b = 80, ngrid = 80L, ndx = 17L,                           degree = 3L, penalty_order = 3L) fit_b_bayes <- bpclm(     m = bloodlead$count, wide_breaks = wb_b,                           a = 0, b = 80, ngrid = 80L, ndx = 17L,                           degree = 3L, penalty_order = 3L,                           niter = 4000L, burnin = 1000L, adapt = 500L,                           shape = "unimodal", seed = 1L) # bloodlead bins are contiguous: x = lower limits, nlast = last bin width. fit_b_ung <- ungroup::pclm(x        = bloodlead$lower,                            y        = bloodlead$count,                            nlast    = 80 - 65,   # last bin is [65, 80)                            out.step = 1) ung_density_b <- as.numeric(fit_b_ung$fitted) / sum(fit_b_ung$fitted) mids_b <- (head(fit_b_pclm$grid, -1L) + tail(fit_b_pclm$grid, -1L)) / 2 plot(mids_b, fit_b_pclm$pi,  type = "l", lwd = 2, lty = 2,      col = "steelblue",      xlab = "Blood-lead concentration (µg/dl)",      ylab = "Density mass per fine cell",      main = "bloodlead: density estimates") lines(mids_b, fit_b_exact$pi, col = "black",     lwd = 2) lines(mids_b, fit_b_bayes$pi, col = "firebrick", lwd = 2, lty = 3) if (have_ungroup) {   lines(seq(0.5, 79.5, by = 1), ung_density_b,         col = "darkgreen", lwd = 2, lty = 4)   legend("topright", bty = "n",          legend = c("pclm_exact", "pclm", "bpclm (unimodal)",                     "ungroup::pclm"),          col = c("black", "steelblue", "firebrick", "darkgreen"),          lwd = 2, lty = c(1, 2, 3, 4)) } else {   legend("topright", bty = "n",          legend = c("pclm_exact", "pclm", "bpclm (unimodal)"),          col = c("black", "steelblue", "firebrick"),          lwd = 2, lty = c(1, 2, 3)) } # How well does each fit reproduce the seven observed bin totals? band_tot <- function(pi_grid, fit_obj) {   as.numeric(fit_obj$C %*% pi_grid) * sum(fit_obj$m) } bl_compare <- data.frame(   observed       = bloodlead$count,   pclm           = round(fit_b_pclm$fitted_counts,  2),   bpclm          = round(band_tot(fit_b_bayes$pi,   fit_b_bayes),  2),   pclm_exact     = round(fit_b_exact$fitted_counts, 6),   ungroup        = if (have_ungroup) {                      # Re-aggregate ungroup's single-year output back to bins                      yhat <- as.numeric(fit_b_ung$fitted)                      bin_id <- findInterval(seq(0.5, 79.5, by = 1),                                             c(bloodlead$lower, 80),                                             rightmost.closed = TRUE)                      round(as.numeric(tapply(yhat, bin_id, sum)), 2)                    } else {                      rep(NA_real_, nrow(bloodlead))                    } ) bl_compare #>   observed  pclm bpclm pclm_exact ungroup #> 1       27 29.96 27.62         27   26.96 #> 2       71 63.63 68.85         71   70.86 #> 3       32 36.04 33.09         32   31.91 #> 4        6  8.12  7.76          6    6.09 #> 5        3  1.13  1.51          3    2.88 #> 6        0  0.11  0.14          0    0.03 #> 7        0  0.01  0.02          0    0.28

title: How pclmbayes compares with the ungroup package
text: Twelve irregular age bands tuberculosis deaths Netherlands, 1907 (illustrative reconstruction; see ?tbdeaths1907). Many counts (n=9440n = 9440) bloodlead, smoothing-induced shrinkage small absolute terms. Recovered age--death density tbdeaths1907.
code: data(tbdeaths1907) wb_t <- with(tbdeaths1907, cbind(lower, upper))  fit_t_pclm  <- pclm(      m = tbdeaths1907$count, wide_breaks = wb_t,                           a = 0, b = 120, ngrid = 120L, ndx = 17L,                           degree = 3L, penalty_order = 3L) fit_t_exact <- pclm_exact(m = tbdeaths1907$count, wide_breaks = wb_t,                           a = 0, b = 120, ngrid = 120L, ndx = 17L,                           degree = 3L, penalty_order = 3L) fit_t_bayes <- bpclm(     m = tbdeaths1907$count, wide_breaks = wb_t,                           a = 0, b = 120, ngrid = 120L, ndx = 17L,                           degree = 3L, penalty_order = 3L,                           niter = 3000L, burnin = 1000L, adapt = 500L,                           seed = 2L) # tbdeaths1907 bins are contiguous; last bin is [100, 120). fit_t_ung <- ungroup::pclm(x        = tbdeaths1907$lower,                            y        = tbdeaths1907$count,                            nlast    = 120 - 100,                            out.step = 1) ung_density_t <- as.numeric(fit_t_ung$fitted) / sum(fit_t_ung$fitted) mids_t <- (head(fit_t_pclm$grid, -1L) + tail(fit_t_pclm$grid, -1L)) / 2 plot(mids_t, fit_t_pclm$pi,  type = "l", lwd = 2, lty = 2,      col = "steelblue",      xlab = "Age (years)",      ylab = "Density mass per single year",      main = "tbdeaths1907: density estimates") lines(mids_t, fit_t_exact$pi, col = "black",     lwd = 2) lines(mids_t, fit_t_bayes$pi, col = "firebrick", lwd = 2, lty = 3) if (have_ungroup) {   lines(seq(0.5, 119.5, by = 1), ung_density_t,         col = "darkgreen", lwd = 2, lty = 4)   legend("topright", bty = "n",          legend = c("pclm_exact", "pclm", "bpclm",                     "ungroup::pclm"),          col = c("black", "steelblue", "firebrick", "darkgreen"),          lwd = 2, lty = c(1, 2, 3, 4)) } else {   legend("topright", bty = "n",          legend = c("pclm_exact", "pclm", "bpclm"),          col = c("black", "steelblue", "firebrick"),          lwd = 2, lty = c(1, 2, 3)) }

title: How pclmbayes compares with the ungroup package
text: want smooth density don’t care preserving bin totals exactly — either pclm() (multinomial) ungroup::pclm() (Poisson) ; pick one whose model matches data-generating story. give similar shapes. want exact preservation wide-bin totals (canonical ungrouping use case) — use pclm_exact(). Neither ungroup::pclm() pclm() . want full posterior uncertainty, optionally shape constraint (unimodal, log-concave, monotonic) — use bpclm(), optionally followed calibrate() enforce exact band totals every draw. ungroup provide . overlapping misaligned wide bins — pclmbayes supports (via rectangle method bin_matrix()). ungroup::pclm() requires contiguous bins. 2D problem (e.g. age × year mortality) — ungroup provides directly (ungroup::pclm2D()). pclmbayes 1D current version. need exposure / population--risk offset — ungroup built ; pclmbayes need model rates externally.

title: How pclmbayes compares with the ungroup package
text: Eilers, P. H. C. (2007). Ill-posed problems counts, composite link model penalized likelihood. Statistical Modelling, 7(3), 239–254. Eilers, P. H. C. Marx, B. D. (1996). Flexible smoothing B-splines penalties. Statistical Science, 11(2), 89–121. Hasselblad, V., Stead, . G. Galke, W. (1980). Analysis coarsely grouped data lognormal distribution. Journal American Statistical Association, 75, 771–778. Lambert, P. Eilers, P. H. C. (2009). Bayesian density estimation grouped continuous data. Computational Statistics Data Analysis, 53(4), 1388–1399. doi:10.1016/j.csda.2008.11.022 Pascariu, M. D., Dańko, M. J., Schöley, J. Rizzi, S. (2018). ungroup: R package efficient estimation smooth distributions coarsely binned data. Journal Open Source Software, 3(29), 937. Rizzi, S., Thinggaard, M., Engholm, G., Christensen, N. C., Jacobsen, R., Vaupel, J. W. Lindahl-Jacobsen, R. (2015). Efficient estimation smooth distributions coarsely grouped data. American Journal Epidemiology, 182(2), 138–147.

title: Bayesian density estimation and exact ungrouping with pclmbayes
text: pclmbayes package estimates smooth latent continuous density data observed wide bins. Two paradigms supported answer two different questions. Density estimation (original paradigm Lambert Eilers, 2009). wide-bin counts treated multinomial sample underlying smooth density, recovered composite-link spline model. fitted bin counts constrained match observed bin counts exactly — smoothness penalty allowed shrink . Exact ungrouping (use case distributing wide-bin counts across fine grid — example, redistributing deaths 5-year age bands across single years age). wide-bin counts treated fixed totals fine-grid counts must sum back exactly. package provides functions . decision tree : pclm() — frequentist density estimation (BIC-selected smoothing). bpclm() — Bayesian density estimation shape constraints (unimodal, log-concave, monotonic) full posterior uncertainty. pclm_exact() — frequentist constrained MAP gives smoothest density consistent observed bin totals exactly. calibrate() — post-hoc projection fit (frequentist Bayesian) onto constraint manifold (exact bin totals). posterior_predict() — credible/prediction intervals fine-cell counts, multinomial sampling noise included appropriate.
code: library(pclmbayes)

title: Bayesian density estimation and exact ungrouping with pclmbayes
text: Seven wide bins blood lead concentration young Puerto Rican children 1974 (Hasselblad et al. 1980, reproduced verbatim Lambert Eilers 2009).

previous_headings: Part 1 — Density estimation (Lambert & Eilers paradigm) > The bloodlead example (paper, §6.1)
title: Bayesian density estimation and exact ungrouping with pclmbayes
text: Frequentist (BIC-smoothed) PCLM fit bloodlead data.
code: fit_f <- pclm(   m             = bloodlead$count,   wide_breaks   = with(bloodlead, cbind(lower, upper)),   a             = 0, b = 80,   ngrid         = 80L,   ndx           = 17L, degree = 3L,   penalty_order = 3L,   select        = "BIC" ) fit_f #> Penalised composite link model (frequentist) #> Call: pclm(m = bloodlead$count, wide_breaks = with(bloodlead, cbind(lower,  #>     upper)), a = 0, b = 80, ngrid = 80L, ndx = 17L, degree = 3L,  #>     penalty_order = 3L, select = "BIC") #>  #> Number of wide bins: 7  | total counts: 139  #> Fine grid:80intervals on (0, 80) #> B-spline basis: K = 20 (degree = 3 ) #> Penalty order r = 3  #> Selected tau = 100 (BIC = 355.79, edf = 2.56) #> Converged = TRUE in 13 iterations.  log-likelihood = -171.582 plot(fit_f)

previous_headings: Part 1 — Density estimation (Lambert & Eilers paradigm) > The bloodlead example (paper, §6.1)
title: Bayesian density estimation and exact ungrouping with pclmbayes
text: Bayesian PCLM fit unimodality constraint 90% credible band.
code: fit_b <- bpclm(   m             = bloodlead$count,   wide_breaks   = with(bloodlead, cbind(lower, upper)),   a             = 0, b = 80,   ngrid         = 80L,   ndx           = 17L, degree = 3L,   penalty_order = 3L,   niter         = 5000L, burnin = 1000L, adapt = 500L,   shape         = "unimodal",   seed          = 1 ) fit_b #> Bayesian penalised composite link model #> Call: bpclm(m = bloodlead$count, wide_breaks = with(bloodlead, cbind(lower,  #>     upper)), a = 0, b = 80, ngrid = 80L, ndx = 17L, degree = 3L,  #>     penalty_order = 3L, niter = 5000L, burnin = 1000L, adapt = 500L,  #>     shape = "unimodal", seed = 1) #>  #> Number of wide bins: 7  | total counts: 139  #> Fine grid:80intervals on (0, 80) #> B-spline basis: K = 20 (degree = 3 ) #> Penalty order r = 3  #> MCMC: niter = 5000, burnin = 1000, thin = 1, kept = 4000 #> Final delta = 1.09  |  acceptance rate = 0.63 #> Shape constraint(s): unimodal  #> Posterior mean tau = 35.7 (sd 59) plot(fit_b)

previous_headings: Part 1 — Density estimation (Lambert & Eilers paradigm) > The bloodlead example (paper, §6.1)
title: Bayesian density estimation and exact ungrouping with pclmbayes
text: paper (§6.1, p. 1395) reports posterior mean 90% credible interval proportion 30 µg/dl risk threshold, mean, standard deviation, selected quantiles. can reproduce chain:
code: prob_above_30 <- apply(fit_b$pi_chain, 1L, function(p) {   1 - approx(x = fit_b$grid, y = c(0, cumsum(p)),              xout = 30, rule = 2)$y }) c(mean   = mean(prob_above_30),   lower  = quantile(prob_above_30, 0.05, names = FALSE),   upper  = quantile(prob_above_30, 0.95, names = FALSE)) #>      mean     lower     upper  #> 0.1495729 0.1067085 0.2005136  summary(fit_b, probs = c(0.20, 0.80)) #> Bayesian penalised composite link model #> Call: bpclm(m = bloodlead$count, wide_breaks = with(bloodlead, cbind(lower,  #>     upper)), a = 0, b = 80, ngrid = 80L, ndx = 17L, degree = 3L,  #>     penalty_order = 3L, niter = 5000L, burnin = 1000L, adapt = 500L,  #>     shape = "unimodal", seed = 1) #>  #> Number of wide bins: 7  | total counts: 139  #> Fine grid:80intervals on (0, 80) #> B-spline basis: K = 20 (degree = 3 ) #> Penalty order r = 3  #> MCMC: niter = 5000, burnin = 1000, thin = 1, kept = 4000 #> Final delta = 1.09  |  acceptance rate = 0.63 #> Shape constraint(s): unimodal  #> Posterior mean tau = 35.7 (sd 59) #>  #> Posterior of mean(Y):  21.7826  (90% CI: 20.5713, 23.0281) #> Posterior of sd(Y):    8.2662  (90% CI: 7.2525, 9.3988) #>  #> Posterior summaries of quantiles (mean and 90% CI): #>    p    mean      lo      hi #>  0.2 14.9881 13.5972 16.2548 #>  0.8 28.0798 26.3564 30.0208

previous_headings: Part 1 — Density estimation (Lambert & Eilers paradigm) > The bloodlead example (paper, §6.1)
title: Bayesian density estimation and exact ungrouping with pclmbayes
text: bpclm() accepts shape = "unimodal", "logconcave", "monotonic", individually vector. constraint imposed via rejection inside MCMC: proposed φ whose induced density violates constraint rejected outright (Eq. 7 paper). density estimation problems underlying distribution known unimodal — like bloodlead example — stabilises fit tails.

title: Bayesian density estimation and exact ungrouping with pclmbayes
text: package ships bloodlead dataset verbatim. tbdeaths1907 dataset illustrative reconstruction historical CBS data used §6.2 Lambert Eilers (2009); see ?tbdeaths1907. Bayesian fit (illustrative) tbdeaths1907 data.
code: data(tbdeaths1907) fit_tb <- bpclm(   m             = tbdeaths1907$count,   wide_breaks   = with(tbdeaths1907, cbind(lower, upper)),   a             = 0, b = 120,   ngrid         = 120L,   ndx           = 17L, degree = 3L, penalty_order = 3L,   niter         = 4000L, burnin = 1000L, adapt = 500L,   seed          = 2 ) plot(fit_tb, xlab = "Age (years)")

what: Why the default fit doesn’t preserve bin totals
title: Bayesian density estimation and exact ungrouping with pclmbayes
text: Lambert–Eilers model wide-bin counts multinomial sample underlying smooth density. smoothness penalty shrinks fitted bin probabilities away empirical proportions, implied counts deviate observed ones. bloodlead data largest deviation 7.4 deaths (roughly 10% shrinkage largest bin): density-estimation use case correct behaviour — count mjm_j noisy realisation, estimating underlying rate. ungrouping use case (wide-bin counts data — say, deaths 5-year age band — want fine-grid breakdown sums back exactly), shrinkage unwanted.
code: data.frame(   lower = bloodlead$lower,   upper = bloodlead$upper,   m_obs = bloodlead$count,   m_fit = round(fit_f$fitted_counts, 2L) ) #>   lower upper m_obs m_fit #> 1     0    15    27 29.96 #> 2    15    25    71 63.63 #> 3    25    35    32 36.04 #> 4    35    45     6  8.12 #> 5    45    55     3  1.13 #> 6    55    65     0  0.11 #> 7    65    80     0  0.01

previous_headings: Part 2 — Exact ungrouping > Two routes to exact preservation
title: Bayesian density estimation and exact ungrouping with pclmbayes
text: Take fit rescale within-bin density γj=mj/m+\gamma_j = m_j / m_+ exactly. within-bin shape comes smooth fit; small kinks may appear bin boundaries. Bayesian draws calibrated one one, constraint enforced every draw.
code: fit_f_cal <- calibrate(fit_f) data.frame(   lower = bloodlead$lower,   upper = bloodlead$upper,   m_obs = bloodlead$count,   m_fit = fit_f_cal$fitted_counts ) #>   lower upper m_obs m_fit #> 1     0    15    27    27 #> 2    15    25    71    71 #> 3    25    35    32    32 #> 4    35    45     6     6 #> 5    45    55     3     3 #> 6    55    65     0     0 #> 7    65    80     0     0

previous_headings: Part 2 — Exact ungrouping > Two routes to exact preservation
title: Bayesian density estimation and exact ungrouping with pclmbayes
text: Solve constrained quadratic program minϕ12ϕ′Pϕs.t.Cπ(ϕ)=m/m+, \min_\phi \tfrac{1}{2}\,\phi'\,P\,\phi \quad \text{s.t.}\quad C\,\pi(\phi) = m / m_+,  returning smoothest density fine grid exactly reproduces wide-bin counts. kinks boundaries.
code: fit_e <- pclm_exact(   m             = bloodlead$count,   wide_breaks   = with(bloodlead, cbind(lower, upper)),   a             = 0, b = 80,   ngrid         = 80L, ndx = 17L, degree = 3L, penalty_order = 3L ) max(abs(fit_e$fitted_counts - fit_e$m))     # ~ 1e-13 #> [1] 3.552714e-15

title: Bayesian density estimation and exact ungrouping with pclmbayes
text: Group simulated single-year mortality counts 5-year bands ungroup back. canonical use case epidemiology demography. RMSE per year sits close irreducible multinomial-within-band sampling SD (nj/6≈20\sqrt{n_j / 6} \approx 20 2500-death band). See validation script dev/validate-ungrouping.R Monte Carlo coverage check.
code: ages <- 0:99 true_p <- 0.10 * dnorm(ages, mean =  2, sd =  3) +           0.06 * dnorm(ages, mean = 25, sd = 12) +           0.84 * dnorm(ages, mean = 75, sd = 12) true_p <- true_p / sum(true_p) N <- 50000L set.seed(42) true_yearly <- as.numeric(rmultinom(1, size = N, prob = true_p)) year_to_band <- (ages %/% 5L) + 1L m_band <- as.numeric(tapply(true_yearly, year_to_band, sum)) wb     <- cbind(seq(0, 95, by = 5), seq(5, 100, by = 5)) fit_pe <- pclm_exact(m = m_band, wide_breaks = wb,                      a = 0, b = 100, ngrid = 100L,                      ndx = 22L, degree = 3L, penalty_order = 3L) yhat <- fit_pe$pi * sum(m_band) sqrt(mean((yhat - true_yearly) ^ 2))         # RMSE per single year #> [1] 18.90452 max(abs(fit_pe$fitted_counts - m_band))      # exact preservation #> [1] 1.818989e-12

title: Bayesian density estimation and exact ungrouping with pclmbayes
text: pclm_exact() deterministic point estimate — uncertainty . Combining bpclm() calibrate() gives chain densities preserve bin totals exactly. helper posterior_predict() turns chain per-cell credible/prediction intervals. Two distinct uncertainty objects supported: type = "rate" — uncertainty smooth latent rate m+πm_+ \pi. Narrow large NN. Use want compare rates across populations. type = "predictive" (default) — uncertainty realised per-cell counts. Within band jj conditional distribution multinomial size mjm_j within-band probabilities πy/γj\pi_y / \gamma_j. Use want plausible breakdowns observed band counts. ```{r posterior-predict-plot, fig.width = 8, fig.height = 4.5, fig.cap = “Posterior predictive (left) rate (right) single-year deaths.”} op <- par(mfrow = c(1, 2), mar = c(4, 4, 2.5, 1)) plot(pp_predictive, show_bins = FALSE, main = “type = ‘predictive’ (90% PI)”, xlab = “Age (years)”, ylab = “Single-year deaths”) lines(ages, true_yearly, col = “firebrick”, lwd = 1.5, lty = 2) plot(pp_rate, show_bins = FALSE, main = “type = ‘rate’ (90% CI)”, xlab = “Age (years)”, ylab = “Expected single-year deaths”) lines(ages, true_yearly, col = “firebrick”, lwd = 1.5, lty = 2) par(op) Cumulative-deaths posterior predictive.
code: fit_b <- bpclm(m = m_band, wide_breaks = wb,                a = 0, b = 100, ngrid = 100L,                ndx = 22L, degree = 3L, penalty_order = 3L,                niter = 4000L, burnin = 1000L, adapt = 500L,                seed = 7) fit_b <- calibrate(fit_b)               # exact band totals on every draw  pp_predictive <- posterior_predict(fit_b, type = "predictive",                                     level = 0.9, seed = 7) pp_rate       <- posterior_predict(fit_b, type = "rate",                                     level = 0.9) The predictive interval (left) is wider because it includes the multinomial sampling noise; it has approximately nominal coverage (≈ 85% empirical at the 90% nominal level in our validation). The rate interval (right) is much narrower because at $N = 50000$ the smooth density is well-determined and the realised counts have substantial sampling noise.  ## Cumulative sums  Because the fine-cell draws preserve every band total exactly, any cumulative sum at a band boundary is exactly known: the credible band closes onto the truth at every multiple of 5. *Within* a band the credible band has positive width, reflecting how the band's counts could have been distributed across single years.   ``` r cdf_chain <- t(apply(pp_predictive$draws, 1L, cumsum)) cdf_mean  <- colMeans(cdf_chain) cdf_lo    <- apply(cdf_chain, 2L, quantile, probs = 0.05, names = FALSE) cdf_hi    <- apply(cdf_chain, 2L, quantile, probs = 0.95, names = FALSE)  plot(ages, cumsum(true_yearly), type = "l", col = "grey40", lwd = 2,      xlab = "Age (years)", ylab = "Cumulative deaths",      main = "Cumulative deaths: truth + 90% predictive band") polygon(c(ages, rev(ages)), c(cdf_lo, rev(cdf_hi)),         col = grDevices::adjustcolor("steelblue", 0.3), border = NA) lines(ages, cdf_mean, col = "black", lwd = 2) abline(v = seq(5, 95, by = 5), col = "grey90", lty = 3) legend("topleft",        legend = c("Truth", "Posterior predictive mean",                    "90% PI", "5-year band boundaries"),        col = c("grey40", "black",                 grDevices::adjustcolor("steelblue", 0.6), "grey90"),        lwd = c(2, 2, 8, 1), lty = c(1, 1, 1, 3), bty = "n")

what: Bayesian Density Estimation from Grouped Continuous Data via the Penalised Composite Link Model
title: Bayesian Density Estimation from Grouped Continuous Data via the Penalised Composite Link Model
text: Bayesian density estimation exact ungrouping grouped continuous data, via penalised composite link model pclmbayes covers two related problems: Density estimation — given wide-bin counts multinomial sample underlying continuous variable, recover smooth latent density (Lambert Eilers, 2009). Exact ungrouping — given wide-bin counts data (e.g. deaths 5-year age bands), distribute across fine grid (e.g. single years age) grid totals sum back exactly band totals. package provides: pclm() / bpclm() — frequentist (BIC) Bayesian fits Lambert–Eilers density model, optional shape constraints (unimodal, logconcave, monotonic). pclm_exact() — constrained MAP gives smoothest density consistent wide-bin counts exactly. calibrate() — post-hoc projection fit (frequentist Bayesian) onto constraint manifold; Bayesian fits, every posterior draw calibrated. posterior_predict() — per-cell credible/prediction intervals ungrouped fine-cell counts, multinomial sampling noise. Datasets bloodlead (Hasselblad et al. 1980) tbdeaths1907 (illustrative reconstruction) used Section 6 paper. full vignette walking .

title: Bayesian Density Estimation from Grouped Continuous Data via the Penalised Composite Link Model
text: specific branch, tag, commit can installed ref argument, e.g. remotes::install_github("ChristK/pclmbayes", ref = "main").

title: Bayesian Density Estimation from Grouped Continuous Data via the Penalised Composite Link Model

code: # wide-bin death counts (e.g. 5-year age bands) and band edges fit  <- bpclm(m, wide_breaks, ...) fit  <- calibrate(fit)                                # exact band totals pp   <- posterior_predict(fit, type = "predictive")   # 90% PI for single-year counts plot(pp)  # or, point-estimate only: fit_e <- pclm_exact(m, wide_breaks, ...)

title: Bayesian Density Estimation from Grouped Continuous Data via the Penalised Composite Link Model
text: latent random variable YY supported (,b)(, b), partitioned II fine-grid intervals equal width Δ\Delta midpoints uiu_i. log πi≈fY(ui)Δ\pi_i \approx f_Y(u_i)\Delta modelled linear combination KK B-splines: πi=eηi∑l=1Ieηl,η=Bϕ \pi_i = \frac{e^{\eta_i}}{\sum_{l=1}^e^{\eta_l}}, \qquad \eta = B\phi identifiability constraint ∑kϕk=0\sum_k \phi_k = 0. rr-th order discrete difference penalty spline coefficients ϕ\phi provides smoothing. wide-bin probabilities γ=Cπ\gamma = C\pi linked observed counts mm multinomial likelihood. Bayesian variant places Gaussian prior Δrϕ\Delta^r\phi precision τ\tau, vague Gamma hyperprior τ\tau. full methodological details see package vignette original paper.

title: Bayesian Density Estimation from Grouped Continuous Data via the Penalised Composite Link Model
text: bloodlead verbatim seven-bin frequency table Hasselblad, Stead Galke (1980) reproduced Lambert Eilers (2009, Section 6.1). “65+” bin bounded 80 µg/dl paper. tbdeaths1907 illustrative reconstruction dataset used Section 6.2 paper. original CBS records unpublished; reconstruction matches published total 9440 deaths plausible early-20th-century age-mortality shape, intended demonstration methodology rather historical inference. See data-raw/make-data.R substitution instructions.

what: Build the bin (composite-link) matrix C — bin_matrix
title: Build the bin (composite-link) matrix C — bin_matrix
text: Builds composite-link matrix \(C\) wide-bin probabilities \(\gamma = C \pi\), \(\pi\) vector fine-grid probabilities. Handles aligned misaligned wide bins via rectangle method.

title: Build the bin (composite-link) matrix C — bin_matrix
code: bin_matrix(wide_breaks, fine_breaks)

title: Build the bin (composite-link) matrix C — bin_matrix
text: wide_breaks Numeric matrix data frame two columns giving lower upper limits wide bin, sorted numeric vector length \(J + 1\) wide bins partition \((, b)\) contiguously. fine_breaks Numeric vector length \(+ 1\) giving fine-grid breakpoints.

title: Build the bin (composite-link) matrix C — bin_matrix
text: \(J \times \) matrix C, C[j, ] proportion fine interval \(\) lies wide bin \(j\).

title: Build the bin (composite-link) matrix C — bin_matrix
code: fine  <- seq(0, 10, by = 0.5) wide  <- seq(0, 10, by = 2) C     <- bin_matrix(wide, fine) dim(C) #> [1]  5 20

what: Bayesian penalised composite link model for grouped data — bpclm
title: Bayesian penalised composite link model for grouped data — bpclm
text: Fits model Lambert Eilers (2009) Markov chain Monte Carlo. sampler Metropolis-within-Gibbs: modified Langevin-Hastings update spline coefficients followed Gibbs draw smoothing precision \(\tau\). Optional shape constraints ("unimodal", "logconcave", "monotonic") imposed rejecting proposals whose induced density violates .

title: Bayesian penalised composite link model for grouped data — bpclm
code: bpclm(m, wide_breaks,       a = NULL, b = NULL,       ngrid = 100L,       ndx = 17L, degree = 3L,       penalty_order = 3L,       niter = 5000L,       burnin = NULL,       thin = 1L,       adapt = 500L,       tau_init = NULL,       a_tau = 1e-4, b_tau = 1e-4,       delta_init = NULL,       target_accept = 0.57,       Sigma = NULL,       shape = NULL,       shape_args = list(),       phi_init = NULL,       seed = NULL,       verbose = FALSE)

title: Bayesian penalised composite link model for grouped data — bpclm
text: m, wide_breaks, , b, ngrid, ndx, degree, penalty_order See pclm. niter Number MCMC iterations (including burn-). Default 5000. burnin Number initial iterations discard. Default floor(niter / 5). thin Thinning interval (default 1). adapt Number additional adaptive-tuning iterations performed main run. Default 500. tau_init Initial value precision \(\tau\). NULL (default) uses BIC-selected value frequentist warm-start fit. a_tau, b_tau Hyperparameters \(\Gamma(, b)\) hyperprior \(\tau\). Defaults 1e-4 . delta_init Initial Langevin step size; default \(1.65^2 / K^{1/3}\). target_accept Target acceptance rate adaptation (default 0.57). Sigma Optional positive-definite \(K \times K\) proposal covariance. NULL (default) uses frequentist warm-start variance-covariance. shape Optional shape constraint(s). subset "unimodal", "logconcave", "monotonic". shape_args Additional arguments shape indicators (currently direction monotonicity). phi_init Optional starting value \(\phi\); defaults frequentist MLE. seed Optional integer reproducibility. verbose Print progress every 10% iterations.

title: Bayesian penalised composite link model for grouped data — bpclm
text: object class "bpclm"; see pclmbayes-package.

title: Bayesian penalised composite link model for grouped data — bpclm
text: Lambert, P. Eilers, P. H. C. (2009). Bayesian density estimation grouped continuous data. CSDA, 53(4), 1388–1399. Roberts, G. O. Rosenthal, J. S. (1998). Optimal scaling discrete approximations Langevin diffusions. JRSS B, 60(1), 255–268.

title: Bayesian penalised composite link model for grouped data — bpclm
code: # \donttest{ data(bloodlead) fit <- bpclm(m = bloodlead$count,              wide_breaks = with(bloodlead, cbind(lower, upper)),              a = 0, b = 80, ngrid = 80, ndx = 17,              niter = 2000, burnin = 500, shape = "unimodal", seed = 1) summary(fit) #> Bayesian penalised composite link model #> Call: bpclm(m = bloodlead$count, wide_breaks = with(bloodlead, cbind(lower,  #>     upper)), a = 0, b = 80, ngrid = 80, ndx = 17, niter = 2000,  #>     burnin = 500, shape = "unimodal", seed = 1) #>  #> Number of wide bins: 7  | total counts: 139  #> Fine grid:80intervals on (0, 80) #> B-spline basis: K = 20 (degree = 3 ) #> Penalty order r = 3  #> MCMC: niter = 2000, burnin = 500, thin = 1, kept = 1500 #> Final delta = 1.09  |  acceptance rate = 0.63 #> Shape constraint(s): unimodal  #> Posterior mean tau = 19.5 (sd 46.7) #>  #> Posterior of mean(Y):  21.7779  (90% CI: 20.5296, 23.0596) #> Posterior of sd(Y):    8.2889  (90% CI: 7.1929, 9.3911) #>  #> Posterior summaries of quantiles (mean and 90% CI): #>     p    mean      lo      hi #>  0.05  9.6024  7.0296 11.6006 #>  0.10 12.0372 10.1232 13.6278 #>  0.25 16.1954 14.9382 17.4892 #>  0.50 20.9888 19.7886 22.4045 #>  0.75 26.4068 24.8010 28.1725 #>  0.90 32.5078 30.0854 35.0971 #>  0.95 36.9349 33.7977 40.6506 plot(fit)  # }

what: Equally-spaced B-spline basis on a fine grid — bspline_basis
title: Equally-spaced B-spline basis on a fine grid — bspline_basis
text: Builds B-spline basis evaluated x, equally-spaced knots extending beyond support give partition--unity interior points (standard P-spline construction Eilers Marx, 1996). number basis functions K = ndx + degree.

title: Equally-spaced B-spline basis on a fine grid — bspline_basis
code: bspline_basis(x, a, b, ndx = 17L, degree = 3L)

title: Equally-spaced B-spline basis on a fine grid — bspline_basis
text: x Numeric vector evaluation points. , b Lower upper limits support \((, b)\). ndx Number equally-spaced knot intervals \((, b)\). degree Spline degree (default 3 cubic B-splines).

title: Equally-spaced B-spline basis on a fine grid — bspline_basis
text: list class "pclm_basis" components B (basis matrix), knots, , b, ndx, degree, K.

title: Equally-spaced B-spline basis on a fine grid — bspline_basis
text: Eilers, P. H. C. Marx, B. D. (1996). Flexible smoothing B-splines penalties. Statistical Science, 11(2), 89–121.

title: Equally-spaced B-spline basis on a fine grid — bspline_basis
code: mids <- seq(0.05, 9.95, by = 0.1) bs   <- bspline_basis(mids, a = 0, b = 10, ndx = 17, degree = 3) dim(bs$B) #> [1] 100  20

what: Calibrate a fitted PCLM to preserve wide-bin totals exactly — calibrate
title: Calibrate a fitted PCLM to preserve wide-bin totals exactly — calibrate
text: Post-hoc renormalisation fitted pclm bpclm object implied wide-bin counts exactly equal observed counts m. wide bin \(j\), fine-grid probabilities \(\pi_i\) (\(\) bin \(j\)) scaled \((m_j / m_+) / \gamma_j\). Calibration assumes wide bins partition support without overlap fine interval belongs exactly one wide bin. overlapping misaligned bins, use pclm_exact instead.

title: Calibrate a fitted PCLM to preserve wide-bin totals exactly — calibrate
code: calibrate(fit, ...)  # S3 method for class 'pclm' calibrate(fit, ...)  # S3 method for class 'bpclm' calibrate(fit, ...)

title: Calibrate a fitted PCLM to preserve wide-bin totals exactly — calibrate
text: fit fitted "pclm" "bpclm" object. ... Currently unused.

title: Calibrate a fitted PCLM to preserve wide-bin totals exactly — calibrate
text: object calibrated pi (, bpclm, calibrated pi_chain, pi_lower, pi_upper). component fitted_counts set equal m exactly, calibrated = TRUE recorded.

title: Calibrate a fitted PCLM to preserve wide-bin totals exactly — calibrate
code: data(bloodlead) fit   <- pclm(m = bloodlead$count,               wide_breaks = with(bloodlead, cbind(lower, upper)),               a = 0, b = 80, ngrid = 80, ndx = 17, degree = 3,               penalty_order = 3) fit_c <- calibrate(fit) max(abs(fit_c$fitted_counts - fit_c$m)) #> [1] 1.421085e-14

title: Difference penalty matrix — diff_penalty
text: Returns matrix \(P = D'D\) gives sum squared \(r\)th-order differences vector length K, .e. $$\sum_k (\Delta^r \phi_k)^2 = \phi' P \phi.$$ rank \(P\) \(K - r\); null space spanned polynomials index degree \(r-1\).

title: Difference penalty matrix — diff_penalty
code: diff_penalty(K, r = 3L, return_D = FALSE)

title: Difference penalty matrix — diff_penalty
text: K Length coefficient vector. r Penalty order (default 3). return_D TRUE, return D (\((K-r) \times K\) difference matrix) P (\(K \times K\) penalty matrix).

title: Difference penalty matrix — diff_penalty
text: Either matrix P list components D P (see return_D).

title: Difference penalty matrix — diff_penalty

what: Latent density on the fine grid — latent_density
title: Latent density on the fine grid — latent_density
text: Returns discrete latent density values \(\pi_i = f_Y(u_i) \Delta\) fine-grid midpoints given vector B-spline coefficients.

title: Latent density on the fine grid — latent_density
code: latent_density(phi, basis)

title: Latent density on the fine grid — latent_density
text: phi Numeric vector B-spline coefficients (length basis$K). basis "pclm_basis" object bspline_basis.

title: Latent density on the fine grid — latent_density
text: Numeric vector length \(\) (number fine grid intervals), summing 1.

what: Frequentist penalised composite link model for grouped data — pclm
title: Frequentist penalised composite link model for grouped data — pclm
text: Fits model Lambert Eilers (2009) penalised maximum likelihood. smoothing parameter \(\tau\) selected chosen information criterion grid candidates supplied.

title: Frequentist penalised composite link model for grouped data — pclm
code: pclm(m, wide_breaks,      a = NULL, b = NULL,      ngrid = 100L,      ndx = 17L, degree = 3L,      penalty_order = 3L,      tau = NULL,      select = c("BIC", "AIC"),      max_iter = 100L,      tol = 1e-7,      phi_start = NULL,      verbose = FALSE)

title: Frequentist penalised composite link model for grouped data — pclm
text: m Numeric vector non-negative wide-bin counts. wide_breaks Either sorted vector \(J + 1\) contiguous wide-bin breakpoints \(J \times 2\) matrix/data frame (lower, upper) limits. , b Lower upper limits support \((, b)\). Defaults smallest largest wide-bin limits. ngrid Number fine-grid intervals \(\). Default 100. ndx Number equally-spaced knot intervals \((, b)\). number B-splines ndx + degree. Default 17. degree B-spline degree. Default 3. penalty_order Order difference penalty. Default 3. tau Smoothing parameter; either scalar vector candidate values. NULL (default) uses internal log-spaced grid length 25. select Information criterion: "BIC" (default) "AIC". max_iter, tol Convergence controls penalised scoring algorithm. phi_start Optional starting coefficient vector. verbose Logical; print iteration progress.

title: Frequentist penalised composite link model for grouped data — pclm
text: object class "pclm"; see pclmbayes-package.

title: Frequentist penalised composite link model for grouped data — pclm
text: Lambert, P. Eilers, P. H. C. (2009). Bayesian density estimation grouped continuous data. CSDA, 53(4), 1388–1399.

title: Frequentist penalised composite link model for grouped data — pclm
code: data(bloodlead) fit <- pclm(m = bloodlead$count,             wide_breaks = with(bloodlead, cbind(lower, upper)),             a = 0, b = 80, ngrid = 80, ndx = 17, degree = 3,             penalty_order = 3) summary(fit) #> Penalised composite link model (frequentist) #> Call: pclm(m = bloodlead$count, wide_breaks = with(bloodlead, cbind(lower,  #>     upper)), a = 0, b = 80, ngrid = 80, ndx = 17, degree = 3,  #>     penalty_order = 3) #>  #> Number of wide bins: 7  | total counts: 139  #> Fine grid:80intervals on (0, 80) #> B-spline basis: K = 20 (degree = 3 ) #> Penalty order r = 3  #> Selected tau = 100 (BIC = 355.79, edf = 2.56) #> Converged = TRUE in 13 iterations.  log-likelihood = -171.582 #>  #> Fitted summary statistics of the latent density: #>   mean = 21.7395, sd = 8.5228 #>      5%     10%     25%     50%     75%     90%     95%  #>  8.6085 11.2664 15.8611 21.2147 26.9966 32.8015 36.6231  #>  #> Goodness of fit (observed vs fitted counts): #>  lower upper obs   exp #>      0    15  27 29.96 #>     15    25  71 63.63 #>     25    35  32 36.04 #>     35    45   6  8.12 #>     45    55   3  1.13 #>     55    65   0  0.11 #>     65    80   0  0.01 plot(fit)

what: Smoothest density that exactly preserves wide-bin counts — pclm_exact
title: Smoothest density that exactly preserves wide-bin counts — pclm_exact
text: Solves equality-constrained problem $$\min_\phi \tfrac{1}{2} \phi' P \phi   \quad \text{s.t.} \quad   C \pi(\phi) = m / m_+,$$ returning smoothest density fine grid exactly reproduces wide-bin counts summed.  Solved sequential quadratic programming constraint linearised step.

title: Smoothest density that exactly preserves wide-bin counts — pclm_exact
code: pclm_exact(m, wide_breaks,            a = NULL, b = NULL,            ngrid = 100L,            ndx = 17L, degree = 3L,            penalty_order = 3L,            max_iter = 200L, tol = 1e-9,            eps_ridge = 1e-8,            phi_start = NULL,            verbose = FALSE)

title: Smoothest density that exactly preserves wide-bin counts — pclm_exact
text: m, wide_breaks, , b, ngrid, ndx, degree, penalty_order See pclm. max_iter, tol, eps_ridge Convergence numerical stability controls SQP iteration. phi_start Optional starting coefficient vector.  Default uses mildly-smoothed pclm fit. verbose Print one line per SQP iteration.

title: Smoothest density that exactly preserves wide-bin counts — pclm_exact
text: object class c("pclm_exact", "pclm"), components pclm, plus lambda (converged Lagrange multipliers) constraint_residual (max \(|\gamma - m / m_+|\)).

title: Smoothest density that exactly preserves wide-bin counts — pclm_exact
code: data(bloodlead) fit <- pclm_exact(m = bloodlead$count,                   wide_breaks = with(bloodlead, cbind(lower, upper)),                   a = 0, b = 80, ngrid = 80, ndx = 17,                   degree = 3, penalty_order = 3) fit$constraint_residual #> [1] 2.775558e-17 max(abs(fit$fitted_counts - fit$m)) #> [1] 3.552714e-15

what: Bayesian density estimation from grouped continuous data — pclmbayes-package
title: Bayesian density estimation from grouped continuous data — pclmbayes-package
text: Implements penalised composite link model (PCLM)   Lambert Eilers (2009) estimating smooth latent   continuous density grouped (binned) observations.   Provides frequentist penalised-scoring fit   (pclm) fully Bayesian fit (bpclm)   using modified Langevin-Hastings sampler adaptive step   size Gibbs step smoothing parameter. Optional   shape-constraint priors (unimodality, log-concavity, monotonicity)   supported.

title: Bayesian density estimation from grouped continuous data — pclmbayes-package
text: Lambert, P. Eilers, P. H. C. (2009). Bayesian density estimation   grouped continuous data. Computational Statistics   Data Analysis, 53(4), 1388–1399. Eilers, P. H. C. Marx, B. D. (1996). Flexible smoothing   B-splines penalties. Statistical Science, 11(2),   89–121. Roberts, G. O. Rosenthal, J. S. (1998). Optimal scaling   discrete approximations Langevin diffusions. Journal   Royal Statistical Society, Series B, 60(1), 255–268. Atchade, Y. F. Rosenthal, J. S. (2005). adaptive Markov   chain Monte Carlo algorithms. Bernoulli, 11(5), 815–828.

what: Posterior or parametric predictive distribution of fine-cell counts — posterior_predict
title: Posterior or parametric predictive distribution of fine-cell counts — posterior_predict
text: Generates draws posterior parametric predictive distribution latent fine-cell (typically single-year) counts. type = "predictive" (default), within wide bin \(j\) conditional distribution fine-cell counts given band total \(m_j\) multinomial size \(m_j\) within-band probabilities \(\pi_y / \gamma_j\).  type = "rate" function returns draws latent smooth rate \(m_+ \pi\).

title: Posterior or parametric predictive distribution of fine-cell counts — posterior_predict
code: posterior_predict(fit, ...)  # S3 method for class 'bpclm' posterior_predict(fit,                   type = c("predictive", "rate"),                   level = 0.9, seed = NULL, ...)  # S3 method for class 'pclm' posterior_predict(fit,                   type = c("predictive", "rate"),                   level = 0.9, n_draws = 2000L, seed = NULL, ...)

title: Posterior or parametric predictive distribution of fine-cell counts — posterior_predict
text: fit fitted "pclm", "pclm_exact" "bpclm" object. type Either "predictive" (default) "rate". See Details. level Credible/prediction level returned interval (default 0.9). n_draws frequentist input objects, number parametric multinomial draws generate (default 2000).  Ignored bpclm input. seed Optional integer reproducibility. ... Currently unused.

title: Posterior or parametric predictive distribution of fine-cell counts — posterior_predict
text: object class "pclm_posterior_predict" – list components draws, mean, median, lower, upper, level, type, grid, grid_mid, wide_breaks, m.

title: Posterior or parametric predictive distribution of fine-cell counts — posterior_predict
text: Two distinct sources uncertainty exist: Rate uncertainty: epistemic uncertainty     smooth underlying rate \(m_+ \pi\).  Captured     bpclm posterior \(\pi\); absent pclm /     pclm_exact point estimates. Predictive uncertainty: multinomial sampling     variability realised fine-cell counts within wide     bin, conditional band total. ungrouping use cases (band counts treated data, fine-cell counts unknown), type = "predictive" usually right object, since gives credible intervals cover realised counts nominal level. wide-bin totals reproduced exactly every draw type = "predictive" (multinomial sampling within band). type = "rate" reproduced exactly input fit calibrated (see calibrate) pclm_exact fit.

title: Posterior or parametric predictive distribution of fine-cell counts — posterior_predict
code: # \donttest{ data(bloodlead) fit <- bpclm(m = bloodlead$count,              wide_breaks = with(bloodlead, cbind(lower, upper)),              a = 0, b = 80, ngrid = 80, ndx = 17,              niter = 2000, burnin = 500, adapt = 300, seed = 1) fit <- calibrate(fit) pp  <- posterior_predict(fit, type = "predictive") plot(pp)  print(pp) #> Posterior predictive (multinomial) draws over 80 fine cells #> Number of draws: 1500   |  level: 0.9  #> Wide bins: 7   |  total counts: 139  #>  #>  cell grid_mid mean lower upper #>     1      0.5 0.19     0     1 #>     2      1.5 0.24     0     1 #>     3      2.5 0.34     0     2 #>     4      3.5 0.41     0     2 #>     5      4.5 0.57     0     2 #>     6      5.5 0.74     0     2 #> ... (74 more cells) # }

title: Shape-constraint indicators — is_unimodal
text: Returns TRUE pi satisfies requested shape property. Used bpclm enforce rejection prior Eq. (7) Lambert Eilers (2009).

title: Shape-constraint indicators — is_unimodal
code: is_unimodal(pi, tol = 1e-12) is_logconcave(pi, tol = 1e-10) is_monotonic(pi,              direction = c("either", "increasing", "decreasing"),              tol = 1e-12)

title: Shape-constraint indicators — is_unimodal
text: pi Numeric vector fine-grid probabilities (discrete density). tol Numerical tolerance. direction monotonicity: "either", "increasing" "decreasing".

title: Shape-constraint indicators — is_unimodal
text: Logical scalar.

title: Shape-constraint indicators — is_unimodal
code: is_unimodal(c(0.1, 0.2, 0.4, 0.2, 0.1)) #> [1] TRUE is_logconcave(dnorm(seq(-3, 3, length.out = 31)) /               sum(dnorm(seq(-3, 3, length.out = 31)))) #> [1] TRUE is_monotonic(c(0.5, 0.3, 0.1), direction = "decreasing") #> [1] TRUE

what: Tuberculosis deaths by age, The Netherlands, 1907 (illustrative) — tbdeaths1907
title: Tuberculosis deaths by age, The Netherlands, 1907 (illustrative) — tbdeaths1907
text: Counts deaths attributed tuberculosis Netherlands 1907 wide age bands. Used Example 6.2 Lambert Eilers (2009) illustrate density estimation heavily grouped mortality data. Total deaths dataset: 9440. Note: original CBS records used paper unpublished. values shipped illustrative reconstruction preserves published total plausible early-20th-century age-mortality shape, intended code testing demonstration methodology, historical inference. See data-raw/make-data.R package source substitution instructions access real data.

title: Tuberculosis deaths by age, The Netherlands, 1907 (illustrative) — tbdeaths1907
code: data(tbdeaths1907)

title: Tuberculosis deaths by age, The Netherlands, 1907 (illustrative) — tbdeaths1907
text: data frame 13 rows (12 age bands plus upper-tail zero) 3 columns: lower, upper (age band, years) count (number deaths).

title: Tuberculosis deaths by age, The Netherlands, 1907 (illustrative) — tbdeaths1907
text: Lambert, P. Eilers, P. H. C. (2009). Bayesian density estimation grouped continuous data. CSDA, 53(4), 1388–1399.

title: Tuberculosis deaths by age, The Netherlands, 1907 (illustrative) — tbdeaths1907
pclmbayes 0.1.0
text: Initial release. Implements method Lambert Eilers (2009) “Bayesian density estimation grouped continuous data” (Computational Statistics Data Analysis 53(4), 1388-1399). Frequentist fit pclm(): penalised scoring BIC/AIC selection smoothing parameter. Bayesian fit bpclm(): modified Langevin-Hastings sampler rotation Cholesky frequentist warm-start variance- covariance, adaptive tuning step size, Gibbs step smoothing precision. Optional shape-constraint priors: unimodality, log-concavity monotonicity (Eq. 7 paper). Example datasets bloodlead (verbatim Hasselblad et al. 1980 / paper) tbdeaths1907 (illustrative reconstruction; see help page caveats). Vignette reproducing worked examples paper.
